Article content: {"entities":{"code":[{"language":"none","code":"# Connect Skill

## Purpose
Read across the vault and surface non-obvious connections 
between ideas that have not been explicitly linked yet.

## Process
1. Read wiki/index.md to understand the full scope
2. Read each wiki entry with attention to what it is 
   near topically but not yet connected to
3. For each non-obvious connection found:
   a. Add a link in both entries
   b. Write a 1-sentence note in each entry explaining 
      what the connection reveals
4. Compile a brief report: what was connected, why it matters

## Quality Standard
Only add connections that are genuinely illuminating.
Not every entry is related. A connection that requires 
a stretch to explain is not worth making.","content":"```
# Connect Skill

## Purpose
Read across the vault and surface non-obvious connections 
between ideas that have not been explicitly linked yet.

## Process
1. Read wiki/index.md to understand the full scope
2. Read each wiki entry with attention to what it is 
   near topically but not yet connected to
3. For each non-obvious connection found:
   a. Add a link in both entries
   b. Write a 1-sentence note in each entry explaining 
      what the connection reveals
4. Compile a brief report: what was connected, why it matters

## Quality Standard
Only add connections that are genuinely illuminating.
Not every entry is related. A connection that requires 
a stretch to explain is not worth making.

```"},{"language":"none","code":"# Synthesize Skill

## Purpose
Produce a synthesis document that combines knowledge 
from multiple wiki entries around a specific question 
or topic. This is not retrieval. It is reasoning.

## Process
1. Identify the topic or question to synthesize around
2. Read all relevant wiki entries (identified by tags and links)
3. Look for:
   - Patterns that appear across multiple sources
   - Tensions or contradictions between sources
   - Gaps where the question is not addressed
   - Connections that are not yet explicitly made
4. Write a synthesis document that:
   - States the central insight clearly in the first paragraph
   - Develops the argument with evidence from specific vault entries
   - Names the tensions honestly
   - Proposes a position where the evidence supports one
5. Save to 06-output/ and link from the relevant wiki entries

## Quality Standard
A synthesis document should contain at least one insight 
that is not in any individual source but emerges from 
the combination. If it is just a summary of multiple 
sources, it is not synthesis.","content":"```
# Synthesize Skill

## Purpose
Produce a synthesis document that combines knowledge 
from multiple wiki entries around a specific question 
or topic. This is not retrieval. It is reasoning.

## Process
1. Identify the topic or question to synthesize around
2. Read all relevant wiki entries (identified by tags and links)
3. Look for:
   - Patterns that appear across multiple sources
   - Tensions or contradictions between sources
   - Gaps where the question is not addressed
   - Connections that are not yet explicitly made
4. Write a synthesis document that:
   - States the central insight clearly in the first paragraph
   - Develops the argument with evidence from specific vault entries
   - Names the tensions honestly
   - Proposes a position where the evidence supports one
5. Save to 06-output/ and link from the relevant wiki entries

## Quality Standard
A synthesis document should contain at least one insight 
that is not in any individual source but emerges from 
the combination. If it is just a summary of multiple 
sources, it is not synthesis.
```"},{"language":"none","code":"# Ingest Skill

## Purpose
Process a new source into the wiki. This means extracting 
key ideas, connecting them to existing knowledge, updating 
relevant wiki entries, and creating new entries where needed.

## Process
1. Read the source completely before doing anything
2. Identify the 3-7 core ideas worth preserving
3. For each idea:
   a. Check whether an existing wiki entry covers it
   b. If yes, update the entry with new information or nuance
   c. If no, create a new entry
4. For each new entry, find at least 2 connections to 
   existing wiki entries and add bidirectional links
5. Update wiki/index.md with any new entries created
6. Add a one-line entry to wiki/hot.md noting what was ingested

## Quality Standard
An ingested source should leave the wiki noticeably richer.
If ingesting a source did not create at least one new connection 
that was not obvious before, look harder.

## What Not to Do
Do not create a summary file that just recaps the source.
The wiki contains ideas, not summaries. Extract ideas and 
integrate them. The source file in 03-sources/ is the record.
The Synthesize Skill","content":"```
# Ingest Skill

## Purpose
Process a new source into the wiki. This means extracting 
key ideas, connecting them to existing knowledge, updating 
relevant wiki entries, and creating new entries where needed.

## Process
1. Read the source completely before doing anything
2. Identify the 3-7 core ideas worth preserving
3. For each idea:
   a. Check whether an existing wiki entry covers it
   b. If yes, update the entry with new information or nuance
   c. If no, create a new entry
4. For each new entry, find at least 2 connections to 
   existing wiki entries and add bidirectional links
5. Update wiki/index.md with any new entries created
6. Add a one-line entry to wiki/hot.md noting what was ingested

## Quality Standard
An ingested source should leave the wiki noticeably richer.
If ingesting a source did not create at least one new connection 
that was not obvious before, look harder.

## What Not to Do
Do not create a summary file that just recaps the source.
The wiki contains ideas, not summaries. Extract ideas and 
integrate them. The source file in 03-sources/ is the record.
The Synthesize Skill

following information:

1. SESSION SUMMARY (3-5 sentences): What we worked on 
   and what was accomplished
2. KEY DECISIONS (bullets): Any decisions made that 
   future sessions should know about
3. OPEN THREADS (bullets): Questions raised that were 
   not resolved, to pick up next time
4. VAULT CHANGES (bullets): What was added, updated, 
   or connected in the vault today
5. NEXT SESSION PRIORITY: The single most important 
   thing to start with next time

Replace the entire file with this new entry. 
Do not append. Each entry should reflect only the 
most recent session.","content":"```
Before we end this session, update wiki/hot.md with the 
following information:

1. SESSION SUMMARY (3-5 sentences): What we worked on 
   and what was accomplished
2. KEY DECISIONS (bullets): Any decisions made that 
   future sessions should know about
3. OPEN THREADS (bullets): Questions raised that were 
   not resolved, to pick up next time
4. VAULT CHANGES (bullets): What was added, updated, 
   or connected in the vault today
5. NEXT SESSION PRIORITY: The single most important 
   thing to start with next time

Replace the entire file with this new entry. 
Do not append. Each entry should reflect only the 
most recent session.
```"},{"language":"none","code":"# Current Projects

```"}]},"plain_text":"Every note-taking system you have ever tried had the same flaw.
It required you to maintain it.
You learned about the Zettelkasten method. You built a folder structure. You set up tags and backlinks and daily notes templates. You spent an afternoon on the setup and felt the satisfying clarity of a system that would finally keep your thinking organized. Then the week got busy. The notes kept coming in. 
The maintenance got left for later. And six months afterward, you had a vault full of disconnected notes that you could not find when you needed them, could not trust to be organized correctly, and could not actually use to think better.
The typical advice is "just build a Zettelkasten" or "use bidirectional links." That is the personal knowledge management equivalent of telling someone to exercise more. Technically correct, practically useless, because the bottleneck has never been the method. It is the maintenance burden.
The system described in this article has no maintenance burden.
You put things in. Claude organizes them, connects them, synthesizes them, and surfaces what is relevant when you need it. The vault rewrites itself. Contradictions reconcile automatically. Every new source you add becomes more valuable because it connects to everything already there. The tenth book produces more insight than the first because there is more to connect to.
The knowledge compounds. Each new source ingested becomes more valuable because it connects to everything already in the vault.
This is the second brain that actually works. Not because the method is better. Because the maintenance is no longer yours to do.

 
Why Every Previous System Failed
Before building the new system, you need to understand precisely what broke every previous one. 
Otherwise, you will repeat the mistake in a new tool.
Traditional second brain systems fail for one structural reason. They treat the human as both the knowledge worker and the knowledge manager simultaneously. You do the work of thinking, reading, and learning. You also do the work of organizing, connecting, tagging, and maintaining. Those are two separate full-time jobs, and asking one person to do both consistently, indefinitely, means one of them gets cut when time gets tight.
Guess which one.
The organizing always loses to the doing. Notes pile up unfiled. Connections that should be made are left unlinked. The vault drifts from structure toward entropy, and the gap between "what I know" and "what the system knows" grows until the system stops being useful.
The second failure mode is the retrieval problem. Even well-maintained vaults suffer from a retrieval architecture that requires you to know what you are looking for before you find it. Search is keyword-based. Navigation is folder-based. You can only surface what you specifically go looking for. The insight that would change your thinking today because it connects something you read in January to something you are working on in October is never surfaced, because you were not looking for it.
A vault that rewrites itself, where every source updates existing pages instead of just appending new ones and contradictions reconcile automatically, is the vault that compounds while you sleep.
The system built here solves both failure modes by design. The maintenance is Claude's job. The connection-making is Claude's job. The surfacing of relevant insight across everything in the vault is Claude's job. Yours is only to add things and to ask questions.

 
The Insight That Makes This Possible
The breakthrough that makes this architecture work came from a specific shift in how to think about AI and knowledge management.
The dominant approach to AI-powered note-taking is retrieval augmented generation: embed your notes as vectors, run semantic search when you need something, and feed the retrieved chunks to the model. It works reasonably well and it has a fundamental problem. The model only sees the chunks it retrieved, not the full picture of what you know. The connections happen at search time, not at ingestion time. The vault does not get smarter as it grows.
In April 2026, Karpathy shared a different approach: use an LLM to pre-compile sources into a structured markdown wiki, then operate on that wiki. Knowledge accumulates. Sessions build on each other. The key insight: LLMs reason well over long structured text. They do not need embeddings to understand a well-organized markdown document.
The shift is from retrieval to compilation. Instead of storing raw notes and searching them, you maintain a wiki that Claude continuously compiles, updates, and cross-references. Every new source you add triggers Claude to update the relevant sections of the wiki, create new entries where they are needed, and link the new material to everything already there.
The result is a vault that the model can reason over directly, without retrieval. Claude reads the compiled wiki at the start of a session and has access to the full body of your organized knowledge, not just the fragments that match a keyword.
Your vault gets smarter as it gets bigger, defying the usual entropy of digital note collections.
This is the foundation everything else builds on.

 
The Four-Layer Architecture
The second brain described here has four layers, each one serving a specific function in the compounding knowledge system.
Layer 1: The Vault Structure
The physical organization of your Obsidian vault. Not arbitrary folders. A deliberate structure that Claude can navigate consistently and that reflects the different types of knowledge you need to manage.
Layer 2: Context Files
The files that tell Claude who you are, what you are working on, and what standards your thinking should meet. These are written once and updated periodically. They are the foundation that makes every Claude session feel continuous rather than cold-started.
Layer 3: Skill Files
The files that tell Claude how to perform specific functions inside the vault. Research ingestion. Synthesis. Connection-making. Review generation. Each skill is a Markdown file that Claude reads before performing a specific task.
Layer 4: The Hot Cache
The file that gets updated at the end of every session with a compressed summary of what was discussed, what was decided, and what matters next. At the end of every session, Claude updates the hot cache with a compact context summary. The next session reads that file first. You never rebuild context.
These four layers work together. The structure provides the home. The context files provide the identity. The skill files provide the behaviors. The hot cache provides the continuity. Together, they produce a system that compounds without maintenance.

 
Module 1: Building the Vault Structure
Create a new Obsidian vault or add this structure to an existing one. The folder names matter because they define the semantic categories Claude uses when deciding where to put things.
 
The inbox is where everything enters without judgment. Nothing gets organized at intake time. You drop things in and Claude sorts them during processing cycles.
The wiki is the compiled knowledge. It is not raw notes. It is structured, interconnected knowledge that Claude maintains continuously.
The sources folder is the raw material. Books you have read. Articles you have saved. Conversations you want to preserve. These are the inputs Claude processes into the wiki.
The skills folder is what makes Claude's behavior consistent and predictable inside your vault.

 
Module 2: The Context Files That Make Claude Know You
The context files are the most important files in the vault. They are what give Claude a persistent understanding of who you are and what you are trying to accomplish, without you having to explain yourself at the start of every session.
about-me.md
 
current-projects.md
This file is updated weekly, not quarterly. It reflects what matters right now, not a stable picture of your work.
 
areas-of-focus.md
 
After writing these three files, every Claude session that begins by reading them is a session where Claude already knows you. The cold-start problem is solved by the context layer.

 
Module 3: The Skill Files That Define Claude's Behavior
Skill files are the behavioral instructions that make Claude's actions inside the vault consistent and high quality. Each skill file is read before Claude performs a specific function.
The Ingest Skill
This skill governs how new sources are processed into the wiki.
Save as 05-skills/ingest.md:
 
This skill creates higher-order knowledge by combining what the vault already knows.
Save as 05-skills/synthesize.md:
 
The Connect Skill
Run periodically to surface connections Claude finds by reading across the whole vault.
Save as 05-skills/connect.md:

 

 
Module 4: The Hot Cache System
The hot cache is the single feature that changes this from a good knowledge system into one that actually compounds over time without maintenance effort.
At the end of every Claude session inside the vault, you ask Claude to update the hot cache. At the start of every new session, Claude reads the hot cache before anything else. The result is a system where every session continues from where the last one left off, carrying all the context and momentum forward.
The hot cache file lives at 02-wiki/hot.md. Its contents are replaced, not appended, at the end of each session.
Template for the hot cache update prompt:
 
The hot cache is what makes the claim "gets smarter without you doing anything" accurate. You are not maintaining the memory. Claude writes it at the end of each session and reads it at the start of the next. The continuity is automatic.

 
The Compounding Mechanism Explained
The compounding works through a specific sequence that repeats every time you add something to the vault.
You drop a new article, book, or note into the inbox. You ask Claude to run the ingest skill on it. Claude reads the source, extracts the core ideas, checks them against existing wiki entries, updates or creates entries, and adds the bidirectional links.
At this point, the new material is not just stored. It is integrated. The ideas from this source are connected to every other relevant idea in the vault. The next time you ask Claude anything related to any of those connected topics, this source's perspective is part of the answer.
AI-driven knowledge management saves knowledge workers 30 to 45 percent of time on information retrieval (McKinsey, 2025). But the deeper return is not time. It is the quality of thinking that emerges when you can reason over five years of organized knowledge instead of scattered notes.
The compounding accelerates as the vault grows because every new connection has more existing knowledge to connect to. The tenth source added creates more new connections than the first source, not fewer, because there are ten times as many existing entries to link to. The vault becomes more valuable per unit of input over time, which is the opposite of how traditional note-taking systems behave.

 
What This Looks Like After 30, 90, and 180 Days
Day 30
The vault has 50 to 100 wiki entries. The hot cache is running. Three to five sources have been fully ingested. The context files are established and working. Most Claude sessions feel continuous rather than cold-started. The system is new enough that you still notice the difference from your old system.
Day 90
The vault has 200 to 400 wiki entries. Pattern recognition has started. Claude surfaces connections between things you ingested two months apart that you would never have noticed manually. The connect skill occasionally finds something genuinely surprising. You have started using the synthesize skill to produce thinking on questions you could not have answered as well before.
Day 180
The promise of a second brain meets a mature toolchain. The vault is a local, searchable, write-capable knowledge system that the model can read, search, create, and modify directly. Obsidian's local-first, Markdown-based approach makes it the ideal foundation for an AI-powered second brain because your notes are plain Markdown files that any AI can access with no vendor lock-in.
The vault has become the starting point for serious thinking rather than a place you go to search for things. When you face a hard decision, you ask Claude to synthesize everything the vault knows about the relevant domain. When you are writing something important, you ask Claude to surface everything in the vault that is relevant to the argument. The vault is no longer a storage system. It is a thinking partner with six months of your organized knowledge behind it.

 
The One Setup You Need This Weekend
The full system described here takes one weekend to build properly.
Saturday morning: create the vault structure and write the three context files. Take time with the context files. They are the most important thing you will build and they only need to be written once.
Saturday afternoon: create the four skill files. Ingest, synthesize, connect, and review. Test each one with a single source to verify Claude uses them correctly.
Sunday: ingest your first five sources. Not your archive. The five most recent things you have read or saved that you actually want in the system. Watch Claude build the initial wiki entries. Read what it connects. Notice what surprises you.
Sunday evening: run the first hot cache update. Read what Claude wrote. That file is the seed of everything that follows.
The first week the vault is thin. The first month it becomes useful. In the first six months, it becomes something you depend on.
The personal knowledge base AI market hit $1.65 billion in 2025 and is growing at 30.3 percent annually toward $6.15 billion by 2030. The people building this infrastructure now will be operating with a knowledge advantage in three years that is essentially impossible to replicate quickly because it requires time to compound.
A vault you start today is six months ahead of a vault you start in six months.
The setup is one afternoon.
The compounding runs forever.
Start this weekend.","title":"How to Build a Second Brain With Obsidian and Claude That Gets Smarter Every Week (Full Course) ","cover_media":"3_2059298746123390976","preview_text":"Every note-taking system you have ever tried had the same flaw.
It required you to maintain it.
You learned about the Zettelkasten method. You built a folder structure. You set up tags and backlinks"}
